text.content: A recommender agent based on learning styles for better virtual collaborative learning experiences

title.plain_text: A recommender agent based on learning styles for better virtual collaborative learning experiences

tables.heading: Results and Discussion
rows: Not Applicable - students belong to Engineering in Foreign Languages School

Learning Style is inferred from Questionnaire. 

Sparsity problem → requires that there is at least one student with each learning tool to have selected a tool as preferrable.
The learning style is continuously updated based on the interactions of the user with the system.

Similarity between items is computed using Jaccard Similarity.

Representation of users used demographic data AND Learning Style as derived from the LS questionnaire.

Shortcuts: based on static information

Suggestions: incorporate learning style and user interactions
{Satisfaction: 12/20 students reported to be likely to use the recommendder again}

Methodologically, the work can be summarized into 2 sections:

1. Obtaining the learning styles from the questionnaire AND a rating of tools based on choices upon registration.

2. Using a Collaborative Filtering approach to make recommendations.

To mitigate the sparsity problem, recommendation matrices are computed seaparately for each learning style.

- Results on accuracy of LS evaluation or Recommendations is not reported.

- Items whose score does not surpass a pre-determined threshold are never shown.

- Cold-Start problem

- Evaluation metrics considered (accuracy)

- Survey evaluation for recommender based on sdatisfaction-

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: More is not necessarily better. Survey data shows that students need more guidance to access materials than when they had less choice. Thus, the authors propose a new collaborative filtering recommender that also finds a student’s learning style. The system produces 2 types of recommendations and the authors showcase an example of its integration in a VLE.

text.content: More is not necessarily better. Survey data shows that students need more guidance to access materials than when they had less choice. Thus, the authors propose a new collaborative filtering recommender that also finds a student’s learning style. blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: Nowadays, there are new forms of education that have not existed before: continuous education, online educations, etc… Meaning that it no longer is knowledge transfer from professor to students. Computer Supported Collaborative Learning (CSCL) is among the most challenging educational opportunities, requiring good SRL skills to be successful.

blocks.text: The authors consider that personalization can be defined as fostering specific learning behaviors. However, e-learning personalization differs from traditional classrooms substantially, bringing forward new challenges related to the fact the e-learning tends to be self-directed.

blocks.text: One popular form of personalization is what is called learner-centered: approaches that make the learner have a defining role in the learning experience (often frowned at by students at the start, but accepted later). Professors in learner centered approaches are not giver’s of content and more coaches or facilitators.

blocks.text: A recommender is defined as an agent that, starting from a very large pool of possible resources, gives a user the approapriate resources and guidance to achieve their learning goals.

blocks.text: The main advantage of a recommender is related with its decisiveness: they make a decision for someone who may not have the expertise or the maturity to know where to go next.

blocks.text: For personalization and recommendations to work, it is pivotal to ensure that there is an effective user model (representation is key). Common attributes used in such representations include personal details, learning experience, goals or style. However, creating a representation that is optimal for a specific application is de facto representation a challenge. Empahsys on Learning Style.

blocks.text: Get an aggregate score for each tool by summing the total similarity between the tool and the others.

blocks.text: The initial matrix is a user-tool matrix, the inverted matrix is a tool-user matrix. This is followed by the computation of a Tool-Tool matrix based on Jaccard Similarity (a similarity matrix basically).

blocks.text: Indexing Learning Styles

text.content: Indexing Learning Styles

rich_text.plain_text: Indexing Learning Styles

blocks.text: So, for students who share a specific learning style, the algorithm works as described previously, which requires classification. The first step for identification is the questionnaire answered by the users. Then, they are classified according to the Index of Learning Styles (ILS): active/reflective, sensing/intuitive, visual/verbal, and sequential/global.

text.content: So, for students who share a specific learning style, the algorithm works as described previously, which requires classification. The first step for identification is the questionnaire answered by the users. Then, they are classified according to the Index of Learning Styles (ILS): active/reflective, sensing/intuitive, visual/verbal, and sequential/global.

Then, they are classified according to the Index of Learning Styles (ILS): active/reflective, sensing/intuitive, visual/verbal, and sequential/global.

blocks.text: To do that, the authors developed a feedback questionnaire that measures: Ease of use, learning style accuracy, usefulness and recommendation accuracy. Most students mentioned to be satisfied with the system in utilization and would recommend its use. However, no actual mention of the performance of the recommender.
